title: The River's Lullaby
content:
Mara lived in a little house on the bank of a wide, gentle river. From her bedroom window she could always hear it, a constant soft murmur, like a song someone had always known. Most nights, that sound helped her drift off to sleep easily. But tonight was different.

Tomorrow was her very first day of school. Mara had never been before. She lay flat on her back, staring at the ceiling. What if she did not know the answers? What if no one wanted to sit with her? What if she got lost?

She crept out of bed and sat on the window seat, watching the river in the moonlight. The water shimmered and moved, always flowing forward, always finding its way around every stone and root in its path. "River," Mara whispered, "how do you always know where to go?"

The river murmured on. Mara closed her eyes and listened carefully. And then, in the way that happens when you are very quiet and the night is very still, she seemed to understand what the river was saying.

"I was not always big," the river seemed to whisper. "I began as a single tiny trickle on the top of a cold mountain. I had never been anywhere before. Every pebble looked like an obstacle too large to pass. I did not know where I was going."

"But I kept moving," the river continued softly. "When a boulder blocked my way, I went around it. When there was a crack in the rock, I found it and slipped through, slowly making it wider. I joined with other little streams and grew stronger. I did not know the way to the sea, but I kept going, and I found it."

Mara opened her eyes and looked at the river flowing quietly in the moonlight. She thought about going around the hard parts instead of stopping. She thought about how even a great river had started small, not knowing the way, but moving forward anyway.

She climbed back into bed and pulled her blanket up to her chin. The river was still singing outside her window, steady and calm. She was still a little nervous. But she decided that nervousness was just excitement with its eyes closed. She shut her eyes and let the river sing her to sleep.
